verbatim: - **Rows 4-5:** Two measurements for VSTESTCD = "SYSBP" were done at visit 1.
parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]

verbatim: - **Row 6:** A third measurement for VSTESTCD = "SYSBP" at visit 1 was a derived record, as indicated by VSDRVFL = "Y".
parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]

verbatim: - **Row 7:** At visit 2, there were no Vital Signs results, as indicated by VSTESTCD = "VSALL" and VSSTAT = "NOT DONE".
parent_section: §4.5.1.3 [Examples of Original and Standard Units and Test Not Done]

verbatim: ### 4.5.2 Linking Multiple Observations
parent_section: §4.5 [Other Assumptions]

verbatim: See Section 8, Representing Relationships and Data, for guidance on expressing relationships among multiple observations.
parent_section: §4.5.2 [Linking Multiple Observations]

verbatim: ### 4.5.3 Text Strings that Exceed the Maximum Length for General Observation-class Domain Variables
parent_section: §4.5 [Other Assumptions]

verbatim: #### 4.5.3.1 Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters
parent_section: §4.5.3 [Text Strings that Exceed the Maximum Length for General Observation-class Domain Variables]

verbatim: Sponsors may have test descriptions (--TEST) longer than 40 characters in their operational database.
parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]

verbatim: Because the --TEST variable is meant to serve as a label for a --TESTCD when a Findings dataset is transposed to a more horizontal format, the length of --TEST is limited to 40 characters (except as noted below) to conform to the limitations of the SAS V5 transport file format.
parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]

verbatim: Therefore, sponsors have the choice to either insert the first 40 characters or a text string abbreviated to 40 characters in --TEST.
parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]

verbatim: Sponsors have the following options for including the full description for these variables in the study metadata:
parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]

verbatim: - If the annotated CRF contains the full text, provide a reference to the aCRF page containing the full test description in the Define-XML document origin definition for --TEST.
parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]

verbatim: - If the annotated CRF does not specify the full text, then the full text should be documented in the Define-XML document and/or other submission materials (e.g., the clinical study data reviewer's guide).
parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]

verbatim: This convention should also be applied to the qualifier value label (QLABEL) in Supplemental Qualifiers (SUPP--) datasets.
parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]

verbatim: **Exception — IETEST in IE and TI domains:** IETEST values in the Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria Not Met (IE) and Trial Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria (TI) domains are exceptions to the 40-character rule and are limited to 200 characters, because these are not expected to be transformed to column labels. Values of IETEST that exceed 200 characters should be described in study metadata as per the convention above.
parent_section: §4.5.3.1 [Test Name (--TEST) Greater than 40 Characters]

verbatim: #### 4.5.3.2 Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables
parent_section: §4.5.3 [Text Strings that Exceed the Maximum Length for General Observation-class Domain Variables]

verbatim: Some sponsors may collect data values longer than 200 characters for some variables.
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: Because of the current requirement for the SAS V5 transport file format, it is not possible to store long text strings using only 1 variable.
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: Therefore, the SDTMIG has defined conventions for storing long text strings using multiple variables.
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: For general observation-class variables and supplemental qualifiers (i.e., non-standard variables, NSVs), the conventions are as follows:
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: - The **first 200 characters** of text should be stored in the parent domain variable and each additional 200 characters of text should be stored in a record in the SUPP-- dataset (see Section 8.4, Relating Non-standard Variable Values to a Parent Domain)
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
cross_refs: §8.4 Relating Non-standard Variable Values to a Parent Domain

verbatim: - When splitting a text string into several records, the text should be **split between words** to improve readability
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: - When the text longer than 200 characters is for a supplemental qualifier, the **first QNAM should describe the NSV without any numeric suffix**
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: - The value for QNAMs for additional text (>200 characters) should contain a **sequential variable name formed by appending a 1-digit integer, beginning with 1**, to the original domain variable name
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: - The value for **QLABEL should be the original domain variable label**:
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: - The reason a digit integer or suffix is not appended to the label is because the long text string represents a single value for a variable; the physical representation (SAS V5 transport file format) does not change the concept described by the label
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: - This is conceptually different from when there are multiple values for a non-result qualifier variable and values are individually stored in SUPP-- (in that case, both QNAM and QLABEL must be uniquely named per Section 4.2.8.3, Multiple Values for a Non-result Qualifier Variable)
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
cross_refs: §4.2.8.3 Multiple Values for a Non-result Qualifier Variable

verbatim: - In cases where the standard domain variable name is already 8 characters in length, sponsors should **replace the last character with a digit** when creating values for QNAM; for example, for Other Action Taken in Adverse Events (AEACNOTH), values for QNAM for the SUPPAE records would have the values AEACNOT1, AEACNOT2, and so on
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: **Example 1 (mh.xpt / suppmh.xpt):** MHTERM was longer than 200 characters and required 2 supplemental qualifier variables for the text that extended beyond what could be represented in the standard variable.
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: mh.xpt
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: suppmh.xpt
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: mh.xpt:
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
§4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: | 1 | 12345 | MH | 99-123 | 6 | 1st ~200 chars of text, split between words |
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: suppmh.xpt:
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
§4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: | 1 | 12345 | MH | 99-123 | MHSEQ | 6 | MHTERM1 | Reported Term for the Medical History | 2nd ~200 chars of text, split between words | CRF |
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: | 2 | 12345 | MH | 99-123 | MHSEQ | 6 | MHTERM2 | Reported Term for the Medical History | last 100 or more chars of text | CRF |
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: **Example 2 (ae.xpt / suppae.xpt):** AEACNOTH was longer than 200 characters, but required only 1 supplemental qualifier for the text that extended beyond what could be represented in the standard variable.
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: ae.xpt
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: suppae.xpt
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: ae.xpt:
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
§4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: | 1 | 12345 | AE | 99-123 | 4 | HEART FAILURE | 1st ~200 characters of text, split between words |
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: suppae.xpt:
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
§4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: | 1 | 12345 | AE | 99-123 | AESEQ | 4 | AEACNOT1 | Other Action Taken | remaining characters of text | CRF |
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: **Example 3 (pr.xpt / supppr.xpt):** PRREAS was longer than 200 characters, but required only 1 supplemental qualifier to represent the remaining text.
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: pr.xpt
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: supppr.xpt
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: pr.xpt:
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
§4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: | 1 | 12345 | PR | 99-123 | 4 | KIDNEY TRANSPLANT |
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: supppr.xpt:
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]
§4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: | 1 | 12345 | PR | 99-123 | PRSEQ | 4 | PRREAS | Reason | 1st ~200 characters of text, split between words | CRF |
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: | 2 | 12345 | PR | 99-123 | PRSEQ | 4 | PRREAS1 | Reason | remaining characters of text | CRF |
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: **Domain-specific conventions for text > 200 characters:**
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: The following domains have specialized conventions: CO (Section 5.1), IE (Section 6.3.4), TS (Section 7.4.2), and TI (Section 7.4.1).
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim:
| Convention | General Observation Class and Supplemental Qualifier Variables | CO.COVAL | TS.TSVAL | TI.IETEST and IE.IETEST |
|------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|----------|----------|------------------------|
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: | Storage | First 200 chars in parent domain variable; each additional 200 chars in a SUPP-- record | First 200 chars in COVAL; each additional 200 chars in a CO domain record linked to COVALn | First 200 chars in TSVAL; each additional 200 chars in a TS domain record linked to TSVALn | If text >200 chars, put meaningful text in IETEST and describe full text in study metadata |
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: | Split rule | Split between words to improve readability | Split between words | Split between words | Not applicable |
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: | Label | QLABEL should be the original domain variable label | Variable labels for COVAL1 to COVALn should be "Comment" | Variable labels for TSVAL1 to TSVALn should be "Parameter Value" | Not applicable |
parent_section: §4.5.3.2 [Text Strings Greater than 200 Characters in Other Variables]

verbatim: ### 4.5.4 Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes
parent_section: §4.5 [Other Assumptions]

verbatim: Because observations may originate from more than 1 source (e.g., investigator, independent assessor), observations recorded in the Findings class include the --EVAL qualifier.
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: For the Interventions and Events observation classes, which do not include the --EVAL variable, all data are assumed to be attributed to the principal investigator.
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: The QEVAL variable can be used to describe the evaluator for any data item in a SUPP-- dataset (see Section 8.4.1, Supplemental Qualifiers -- SUPP-- Datasets), but is not required when the data are objective.
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: For observations that have primary and secondary evaluations of specific qualifier variables, sponsors should put data from the primary evaluation into the standard domain dataset and data from the secondary evaluation into the Supplemental Qualifier datasets (SUPP--).
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: Within each SUPP-- record, the value for QNAM should be formed by appending a "1" to the corresponding standard domain variable name.
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: In cases where the standard domain variable name is already 8 characters in length, sponsors should replace the last character with a "1" (incremented for each additional attribution).
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: **Example:** An adjudication committee evaluated an adverse event.
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: The evaluations of the adverse event by the primary investigator were represented in the standard AE dataset.
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: The evaluations of the adjudication committee were represented in SUPPAE.
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: See Section 8.4, Relating Non-standard Variable Values to a Parent Domain.
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]
cross_refs: §8.4 Relating Non-standard Variable Values to a Parent Domain

verbatim: Note that the QNAM for the "Relationship to Non-study Treatment" supplemental qualifier is AERELNS1, rather than AERELNST1, because AERELNST is already 8 characters in length.
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: suppae.xpt:
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]
§4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: | 1 | 12345 | AE | 99-123 | AESEQ | 3 | AESEV1 | Severity/ Intensity | MILD | CRF | ADJUDICATION COMMITTEE |
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: | 2 | 12345 | AE | 99-123 | AESEQ | 3 | AEREL1 | Causality | POSSIBLY RELATED | CRF | ADJUDICATION COMMITTEE |
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: | 3 | 12345 | AE | 99-123 | AESEQ | 3 | AERELNS1 | Relationship to Non-study Treatment | Possibly related to aspirin use | CRF | ADJUDICATION COMMITTEE |
parent_section: §4.5.4 [Evaluators in the Interventions and Events Observation Classes]

verbatim: ### 4.5.5 Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data
parent_section: §4.5 [Other Assumptions]

verbatim: For assessments of clinical significance when the overall interpretation is in the domain, use the --CLSIG (Clinically Significant) variable on the record that contains the overall interpretation or a particular result.
parent_section: §4.5.5 [Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data]

verbatim: For example, EGCLSIG = "Y" indicates that an ECG result of "ATRIAL FIBRILLATION" was clinically significant.
parent_section: §4.5.5 [Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data]

verbatim: Separate from clinical significance are results of "NORMAL" or "ABNORMAL", or lab values that are out of normal range.
parent_section: §4.5.5 [Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data]

verbatim: Examples of the latter include:
parent_section: §4.5.5 [Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data]

verbatim: - An ECG test with EGTESTCD = "INTP" (which addresses the ECG as a whole) should have a result of "NORMAL" or "ABNORMAL". A record for EGTESTCD = "INTP" may also have EGCLSIG indicating whether the result is clinically significant.
parent_section: §4.5.5 [Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data]

verbatim: - A record for a vital signs measurement (e.g., systolic blood pressure) or a lab test (e.g., hematocrit) that contains a measurement may have a normal range and a normal range indicator. It could also have --CLSIG indicating whether the result was clinically significant.
parent_section: §4.5.5 [Clinical Significance for Findings Observation Class Data]

verbatim: ### 4.5.6 Supplemental Reason Variables
parent_section: §4.5 [Other Assumptions]

verbatim: The SDTM general observation classes include the --REASND variable to submit the reason a response is not present (a result in a findings class or an --OCCUR value in an events or interventions variable).
parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]

verbatim: For Events and Interventions domains where prespecified occurrences have a reason for the "Y" or "N" value of --OCCUR, the reason can be represented with the variable --REASOC.
parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]

verbatim: However, sponsors sometimes collect the reason that something was done.
parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]

verbatim: For the Interventions general observation class, --INDC is available to represent the medical condition for which the intervention was given, and --ADJ is available to represent the reason for a dose adjustment.
parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]

verbatim: For the Findings general observation class, --REASPF is available to represent the reason a test was performed.
parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]

verbatim: If the sponsor collects a reason for performing an activity represented in an Events domain where the topic is not a medical condition, or a reason for an intervention other than a medical indication, the reason can be represented in the SUPP-- dataset (as described in Section 8.4.1, Supplemental Qualifiers -- SUPP-- Datasets) using the supplemental qualifier with QNAM of "--REAS" listed in Appendix C1, Supplemental Qualifiers Name Codes.
parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]
cross_refs: Appendix C1 Supplemental Qualifiers Name Codes

verbatim: If multiple reasons are reported, refer to Section 4.2.8.3, Multiple Values for a Non-result Qualifier Variable.
parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]
cross_refs: §4.2.8.3 Multiple Values for a Non-result Qualifier Variable

verbatim: For example, if the sponsor collected the reason for admission to a nursing home was for rehabilitation, a SUPPHO record might be populated as follows.
parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]

verbatim: suppho.xpt:
parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]
§4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]

verbatim: | 1 | 12345 | HO | 99-123 | HOSEQ | 3 | HOREAS | Reason | REHABILITATION | CRF |
parent_section: §4.5.6 [Supplemental Reason Variables]

verbatim: ### 4.5.7 Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events
parent_section: §4.5 [Other Assumptions]

verbatim: Interventions (e.g., concomitant medications) and events (e.g., medical history) can generally be collected in 2 different ways, by recording either verbatim free text or the responses to a prespecified list of treatments or terms.
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: Because the method of solicitation for information on treatments and terms may affect the frequency at which they are reported, whether they were prespecified may be of interest to reviewers.
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: The --PRESP variable is used to indicate whether a specific intervention (--TRT) or event (--TERM) was solicited.
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: It has controlled terminology of "Y" (for "Yes") or a null value.
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: It is a permissible variable, and should only be used when the topic variable values come from a prespecified list.
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: Questions such as "Did the subject have any concomitant medications?" or "Did the subject have any medical history?" should not have records in an SDTM domain because (1) these are not valid values for the respective topic variables of CMTRT and MHTERM, and (2) records whose sole purpose is to indicate whether or not a subject had records are not meaningful.
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: The --OCCUR variable is used to indicate whether a prespecified intervention or event occurred or did not occur.
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: It has controlled terminology of "Y" and "N" (for "Yes" and "No").
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: It is a permissible variable and may be omitted from the dataset if no topic-variable values were prespecified.
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: If a study collects both prespecified interventions and events as well as free-text events and interventions, the value of --OCCUR should be "Y" or "N" for all prespecified interventions and events, and null for those reported as free text.
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: The --STAT and --REASND variables can be used to provide information about prespecified interventions and events for which there is no response (e.g., investigator forgot to ask).
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: As in Findings, --STAT has controlled terminology of NOT DONE.
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]
§4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: | Spontaneously reported event occurred | | | |
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: | Prespecified event occurred | Y | Y | |
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: | Prespecified event did not occur | Y | N | |
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: | Prespecified event has no response | Y | | NOT DONE |
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: Collection design may prespecify specific treatments or terms or prespecify a group of treatments or terms (e.g., "Was a short-acting bronchodilator taken in the 8 hours prior to spirometry?").
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: When an explicit question asks about the occurrence of a group of interventions or events, the group value should be represented in --TRT or --TERM, respectively.
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: Refer to the standard domains in the Events and Interventions general observation classes for additional assumptions and examples.
parent_section: §4.5.7 [Presence or Absence of Prespecified Interventions and Events]

verbatim: ### 4.5.8 Accounting for Long-term Follow-up
parent_section: §4.5 [Other Assumptions]

verbatim: Studies often include long-term follow-up assessments to monitor a subject's condition.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: Use cases include studies in terminally ill populations that periodically assess survival and studies involving chronic disease that include follow-up to assess relapse.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: Long-term follow-up is often conducted via telephone calls rather than clinic visits.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: Regardless of the method of contact, the information should be stored in the appropriate topic-based domain.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: Overall study conclusion in the Disposition (DS) domain occurs once all contact with the subject ceases.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: If a study has a clinical treatment phase followed by a long-term follow-up phase, these 2 segments of the study can be represented as separate epochs within the overall study, each with its own epoch disposition record.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: The following example illustrates the recommended SDTM approach to storing these data.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: An oncology study encompasses 2 months of clinical treatment and assessments followed by once-monthly telephone contacts.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: The contacts continue until the subject dies.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: During the telephone contact, the investigator collects information on the subject's survival status and medication use.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: The answers to certain questions may trigger other data collection.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: For example, if the subject's survival status is "dead", then this indicates that the subject has ceased participation in the study, so a study discontinuation record would need to be created.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: In SDTM, the data related to these follow-up telephone contacts should be stored as follows:
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: 1. Concomitant medications reported during the contact should be stored in the CM domain.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: 2. The subject's survival status should be stored in the Subject Status (SS) domain.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: 3. The disposition of the subject at the time of the final follow-up contact should be stored in DS. Note that overall study conclusion is the point where any contact with the subject ceases, which in this example is also the conclusion of long-term follow-up. The disposition of the subject at the conclusion of the 2-month clinical treatment phase would be stored in DS as the conclusion to that epoch. Long-term follow-up would be represented as a separate epoch. Therefore, in this example the subject could have 3 disposition records in DS, with both the follow-up epoch disposition and the overall study conclusion disposition being collected at the final telephone contact. See Section 6.2.4, Disposition, for detailed assumptions and examples.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: 4. If the subject's survival status is "dead", the Demographics (DM) variables DTHDTC and DTHFL must be appropriately populated.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: 5. The long-term follow-up phase would be represented in Trial Arms (TA), Trial Elements (TE), and Trial Visits (TV).
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: 6. The contacts would be recorded in Subject Visits (SV) and Subject Elements (SE) consistent with the way they are represented in TV and TE.
parent_section: §4.5.8 [Accounting for Long-term Follow-up]

verbatim: ### 4.5.9 Baseline Values
parent_section: §4.5 [Other Assumptions]

verbatim: The variable --LOBXFL was introduced in SDTMIG v3.3 to address the need for a consistent definition of a value that can serve as a reference with which to compare post-treatment values.
parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]

verbatim: This generic definition approximates the concept of baseline and can be used to calculate post-treatment changes.
parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]

verbatim: In domains where --BLFL was expected, its core value was changed from expected to permissible and the variable --LOBXFL was added to contain the consistent definition, with a core value of expected.
parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]

verbatim: In domains where --BLFL was permissible, the variable --LOBXFL was added with a core value of permissible.
parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]

verbatim: The following table shows a set of similar flag variables and their usage across the SDTM and ADaM.
parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]

verbatim:
| Variable | Structure Where It Is Defined | Requirement in That Structure | Definition | Intended Use |
|----------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|------------|--------------|
parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]

verbatim: | --LOBXFL | SDTM Findings | Expected or Permissible | Last non-missing value prior to RFXSTDTC (operationally derived) | Consistent pre-treatment reference value baseline for use across all studies and sponsors |
parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]

verbatim: | ABLFL | ADaM BDS | Conditionally Required | Flags the record that is the source of the baseline value for a given parameter specified in the statistical analysis plan (SAP; may differ both across and within studies and datasets) | Baseline for ADaM analysis as specified in the SAP |
parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]

verbatim: | --BLFL | SDTM Findings | Permissible (formerly Expected in some domains) | A baseline defined by the sponsor (could be derived in the same manner as --LOBXFL or ABLFL, but is not required to be) | Any sponsor-defined baseline use |
parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]

verbatim: As shown in the table, each variable serves a specific need.
parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]

verbatim: The SDTM variable --LOBXFL (and/or --BLFL, if used) can be copied to ADaM for traceability and transparency, but only the ADaM variable ABLFL would be used to signify baseline for analysis.
parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]

verbatim: The content of --LOBXFL and ABLFL will be exactly the same when the SAP specifies that the baseline used for analysis is the last non-missing value prior to RFXSTDTC.
parent_section: §4.5.9 [Baseline Values]
